principle: Out-of-market event-driven strategies cannot beat BnH PnL% unless position sizing is large enough to compensate for low market exposure (<=10% of time in market requires >= 10x per-trade edge vs BnH return rate)

Simple MA regime filters overfit to the number of bear/bull cycles in training. One clean bear market provides only 1 data point — not enough to validate the filter. A strategy that makes 1 correct macro call in training is likely to fail when the validation period has a different macro structure (e.g., choppy near the threshold).

Per-asset trend gating is more robust than global regime filtering: individual assets crossing below their MA allows partial portfolio exposure rather than forcing all-in/all-out binary transitions. This reduces threshold-bounce fragility and maintains exposure diversity across assets with different drawdown timings.

Cross-sectional momentum + trend gating is the combination that works: momentum alone underperforms BnH (it chases losers in correlated bear markets), trend gating alone overfits (single MA call), but their combination concentrates in bull leaders while avoiding confirmed downtrends.

Rebalance frequency is a regime-dependent tradeoff: shorter intervals compound faster in trending markets but generate costly churn in range-bound markets. For weekly-scale momentum strategies, bi-weekly rebalancing improves OOS performance in choppy regimes at the cost of lower in-sample returns during strong trends.

Momentum lookback should be 2-4x the rebalance interval. A lookback much longer than the rebalance period (e.g., 8w lookback with 2w rebalance) creates lag at trend reversals: the signal still shows positive momentum from prior weeks while the asset is already declining, keeping the strategy in losing positions longer.

Reactive vol-based sizing (EWMA) hurts gate-protected momentum strategies: EWMA vol is elevated during post-crash recoveries, causing the strategy to enter recoveries with smaller positions and capture less upside. The trend gate already handles bear-market exits; vol scaling at the margin penalizes recovery re-entries. Vol scaling may benefit absolute-return strategies but not momentum strategies with a trend-exit gate.
